repo.description: Vim-fork focused on extensibility and usability
repo.name: neovim

body:
This PR closes #24788 by implementing a single `--remote` workflow.

This is now possible without [mhinz/neovim-remote](https://github.com/mhinz/neovim-remote)

```lua
vim.env.GIT_EDITOR = "nvim -p --server $NVIM --remote"
```

I used https://github.com/neovim/neovim/pull/18414 as reference.

This is my first contribution.

## Summary
- Make `--remote` the wait behavior by default (block until opened buffers are unloaded on the target server).
- Add `+async` as the explicit non-blocking opt-out.
- Execute `+{cmd}` and `-c` remotely (including arguments around `--remote`) and forward command output to stdout.
- Use `rpcnotify` for quit-like commands to avoid peer-close request errors.

## Implementation
- The client tracks pending remote buffers (`remote_wait_buf_count`) and waits in the event loop.
- The server registers per-buffer `BufUnload` notifications and a `VimLeave` fallback to release any remaining waiters.
- Wait completion is signaled via the internal RPC event `nvim_remote_wait_done_event`.
- Dispatch quit-like commands (`q`, `qa`, `wq`, `xall`, etc.) via `rpcnotify` and stop command iteration immediately, avoiding request/response races on channel close.

comment_data.body:
? No, it should just be a `:async` command "modifier" that works similarly to other command "modifiers" like `:exe`, `:keeppatterns`, `:filter`, etc. Except `:async` by default will just schedule the command via `vim.schedule` or something like that.

There is nothing special about `:async` whether it's called from `--remote` or interactively via the `:` cmdline.

comment_data.body: forward declarations are autogenerated, this is not needed in our codebase...

this looks a bit strange, like `:async` is special-casing the `{file}` somehow.

if the caller cares about "opening a file without waiting", then it should do `+'async edit {file}`. we shouldn't try to special-case how files are opened.

comment_data.body:
I changed `_cs_remote()` to use `v:argv` directly instead of having `remote_request()` build and pass a duplicate argv array from C. That matches the source used by `:restart`.

I kept passing `remote_arg_idx`, because C already knows which argv entry selected remote mode.

I did not switch file collection to `v:argf`. From what I found, that would change behavior because `v:argf` contains all startup file args, while `--remote` currently scopes remote files to args after `--remote`. For example:

```sh
nvim local.txt --server /tmp/nvim.sock --remote remote.txt
```

Current behavior opens only `remote.txt` remotely. Using `v:argf` would also open `local.txt` remotely.

`v:argf` also drops option/command args, so it cannot preserve ordered `+cmd` / `-c` handling around `--remote`, for example:

```sh
nvim '+call setline(1, "first")' --server /tmp/nvim.sock --remote remote.txt -c 'echo getline(1)'
```

That seems to still need argv-level ordering and `--` boundary handling, so I kept the remaining Lua parsing.

Probably there's a better solution for this.

comment_data.body:
> Current behavior opens only `remote.txt` remotely. Using `v:argf` would also open `local.txt` remotely.

That's fine. I don't see why we need to support opening files locally *and* remotely in the same CLI invocation. So let's use `v:argf` and document that "all file args" will go to the `--remote` server.

Something to keep in mind for this entire topic: anyone that wants to do complex things locally *and* remotely already has the option of starting a local `nvim` and running arbitrary scripts via `nvim -l` or `:lua`. So (correct me if wrong) there is generally no need to try to be clever.

comment_data.body:
It now consumes everything after `--remote` except files.

If remote fail is falls back to local.

in this case:

```bash
nvim '+echo "local"' --remote '+echo "remote"'
```

If no server is found both `+echo "local"` and `+echo "remote"` would be executed locally.

If remote succeeds commands before the `--remote` flag are effectively ignored.

comment_regular_data.body:
I tried to [make it clear](https://github.com/neovim/neovim/issues/24788#issuecomment-1717489228) that we don't want a lot of `--remote-x` flags. Instead:

- a single `--remote` flag that causes all `-c` / `+` commands to operate on the remote
    - they always "wait" by default
    - they always return output
- to achieve "async" (i.e. "no wait"), introduce a `:async` command or something like that.

At a glance this looks like the right "shape". We can consider eager-merging this after Nvim 0.12 release (mid March), though I (and hopefully others) will need to review more closely.

- If this is AI-assisted, please double-check code, docs, and PR descrption to REMOVE verobsity and MAXIMIZE clarity. Avoid redundant code, redundant/duplicate tests, blathering explanations, etc. Explicitness and clarity are good but verbosity is not.
- Please plan to rebase this every week or 2 as we work towards 0.12 release.
